content:
6 min read
Just now

--

GPT-5-class models are already smart enough. The real challenge is building the system that catches them when they’re wrong.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

An LLM is just one component. Reliability comes from the system around it. (generated using gemini pro)
At 2 a.m., my AI customer support agent told a customer their refund had been processed.
It hadn’t.
The next morning, I woke up to an email with the subject line:
URGENT???
Three question marks. Never a good sign.
I opened the conversation expecting to find a bug in my workflow.
Instead, I found something worse.
The model had confidently told a customer their refund was complete even though no refund had ever been initiated.
Nothing had crashed.
No error had been thrown.
No monitoring alert had fired.
The AI simply generated a sentence that sounded exactly like something a competent support agent would say… and moved on as if it were true.
I stared at the screen for a minute before one realization hit me:
I’d been treating the LLM as the system.
It isn’t.
It’s just one very talkative, very confident employee inside a much larger operation.
And if you don’t build the operation around it, that employee will eventually improvise when nobody is watching.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Without validation, every response is trusted — even when it’s wrong. (generated using gemini pro)
Why this matters now
Every founder, indie hacker, and product team is racing to add AI to their products.
Chatbots.
Agents.
Automated workflows.
AI has gone from a competitive advantage to an expected feature.
But here’s the uncomfortable reality:
Most AI products are still held together with a prompt and a prayer.
The gap between a fun demo and software you’d trust with real customers, real money, or real business decisions isn’t a smarter model.
GPT-5-class models are already remarkably capable.
The gap is structural.
It’s the invisible plumbing that catches the model when it inevitably says something malformed, inconsistent, or simply wrong.
That plumbing is not the prompt is what separates a demo from a dependable product.

The myth almost everyone believes
When I first started building AI workflows, I kept waiting for the next model release.
Surely the next version would hallucinate less.
Surely the next update would be reliable enough that I could remove all the guardrails.
That update never came.
Because reliability was never really an intelligence problem.
It was a systems problem.
Even the smartest person you know will occasionally misunderstand a question, forget a detail, or answer too quickly.
LLMs aren’t different.
They’re just faster.
And they’re remarkably confident while being wrong.
Imagine inviting an incredibly well-read guest speaker onto your podcast every single day.
They’re insightful.
They’re articulate.
They’re funny.
But every now and then, they’ll misremember a statistic, answer a different question than the one you asked, or confidently speculate about something they don’t actually know.
Would you broadcast every sentence live without an editor?
Probably not.
You’d have a producer.
A script.
A fact-checking process.
A mute button if something went sideways.
Yet that’s exactly how many AI products are built today.
The model speaks.
The customer reads it.
Nobody checks anything in between.

So what does reliable AI actually look like?
Surprisingly, it starts with something many developers think is boring.
A contract.
Not between people.
Between your AI and the rest of your system.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

JSON Schema turns unpredictable AI output into a contract your application can safely trust. (generated using Gemini Pro)
JSON Schema: the bouncer at the door
The single highest-leverage improvement I ever made wasn’t a better prompt.
It was stopping the model from responding; however, it wanted.
Instead of saying:
“Tell me the customer’s order status and next steps.”
I defined exactly what a valid response looked like.
Specific fields.
Specific data types.
Approved values.
Nothing extra.
For example:

• order_status must be one of the five approved values.

• next_action must be under 200 characters.

• Required fields cannot be omitted.

• Unexpected properties aren’t allowed.
The model is still free to generate the best answer it can.
But only inside those boundaries.
That completely changed how I thought about structured outputs.
JSON Schema isn’t a formatting preference. It’s a contract.
The model can improvise inside the walls of that contract.
It can’t leave the building.
If the response doesn’t satisfy the schema, it never reaches a customer.
You’re no longer asking the model to behave.
You’re designing a system where bad behavior can’t quietly slip through.
That one shift dramatically reduces an entire category of production failures.

Detection isn’t enough
A schema tells you something is wrong.
It doesn’t tell you what to do next.
That’s where orchestration becomes the real product.

n8n: giving your AI a nervous system
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A production AI workflow validates, retries, logs, and escalates before responding to users. (generated using Gemini Pro)I stopped thinking about n8n as an automation platform.
Instead, I started seeing it as the nervous system wrapped around the model’s brain.
The LLM generates an idea.
The nervous system decides whether that idea is safe to act on.
It validates outputs.
Routes decisions.
Retries failures.
Escalates uncertainty.
Just like your own nervous system pulls your hand away from a hot stove before your brain consciously processes pain, a well-designed workflow reacts before a bad response ever reaches a user.
In practice, that nervous system is surprisingly simple:

• Validation checks every response against your JSON Schema.

• Retry logic automatically asks the model to fix only the fields that failed validation.

• Fallback branches hand difficult cases to a human or return a safe default instead of retrying forever.

• Logging records every validation failure so recurring weaknesses become visible instead of becoming tomorrow morning’s angry support email.
None of these nodes is exciting.
They won’t go viral on social media.
But they’re the difference between a system that degrades gracefully and one that confidently invents refunds that never happened.
The LLM shouldn’t decide whether it deserves another chance.
Your workflow should.

Human-in-the-loop isn’t admitting failure
I used to think every human intervention meant my automation had failed.
Now I think the opposite.
A circuit breaker isn’t evidence that your electrical system is poorly designed.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Human review isn’t a backup plan; it’s a safety mechanism for high-risk decisions. (generated using Gemini Pro)It’s the reason your house doesn’t burn down.
The same principle applies to AI.
If your workflow automatically escalates low-confidence outputs, repeated validation failures, or high-risk decisions, refunds, legal language, financial transactions, or medical information that isn’t a weakness.
It’s resilience.
You’re acknowledging that intelligence and reliability aren’t the same thing.
And that’s exactly what makes the overall system trustworthy.

Test the seams, not just the model
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Don’t just test prompts. Test every point where AI interacts with the rest of your system. (generated using Gemini Pro)Most developers spend their testing time refining prompts.
Very few test what happens after the prompt succeeds.
That’s where production systems usually fail.
A field that’s normally a string suddenly becomes a number.
A timestamp changes format.
An empty array appears where downstream code expected one object.
The prompt didn’t fail.
The integration did.
Before shipping any AI workflow, deliberately feed it ugly inputs.
Edge cases.
Malformed requests.
Incomplete data.
Contradictory instructions.
Not because you’re trying to prove the model is smart.
Because you’re trying to prove your system stays standing when it isn’t.
The smartest part of most production AI systems isn’t the model.
It’s everything built on the assumption that the model will eventually be wrong.

One thing you can do today
Choose the AI workflow you rely on most.
Keep it small.
Write a JSON Schema with just three or four required fields.
Add validation before your application trusts the output.
That’s it.
Twenty minutes of work.
A surprisingly large increase in reliability.
And quite possibly one fewer 2 a.m. email filled with question marks.

The quiet truth
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The difference between a demo and a production system isn’t a smarter model it’s better engineering.We spend an incredible amount of time asking:
“Which model should I use?”
A better question is:
What happens the first time my model is wrong?
Because it will be.
Every AI system eventually encounters an input it wasn’t prepared for.
That’s inevitable.
The teams quietly shipping reliable AI aren’t waiting for perfect models.
They’re building systems that expect imperfection and are designed to catch it before anyone else notices.
That’s the real difference between an AI demo…
and an AI product.
